narrative: Your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model's prediction toward expecting you to fail the bar exam on your first try, since it's notably lower than the typical 3.21 average among students in the dataset. This low GPA signaled to the model a weaker foundation in academic performance compared to others who tend to pass. In contrast, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 6—slightly better than the average of 5.53—worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success and showing some improvement over time. Your middle family income bracket also pulled against you somewhat, as it aligned with patterns where students from similar backgrounds faced higher risks of failing the bar. As a Black male applicant planning full-time studies with an LSAT score of 36—close to the 36.70 average—the model weighed these together and estimated a 60% chance you'd fail the bar exam first time around. Because law schools rely on this prediction to gauge future success, your application was rejected to minimize the risk of investing in someone unlikely to pass the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 5 was around average, but it didn't outweigh the dominant drag from your undergrad grades. Overall, your profile compared to typical students by having solid test scores and later academic gains, yet the early GPA gap made you riskier in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a dedicated full-time student—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking undergrad courses or a post-bac program to boost your GPA, as even small improvements there could shift future predictions dramatically. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources tailored for students from underrepresented backgrounds like yours to build proven success signals. Admissions teams value persistence, so reapplying after strengthening these areas could open doors—keep pushing, you've got strengths to build on.
